titleStmt.title: Letter from John Chamberlain to Dudley Carleton, 8 December 1598

publicationStmt.p: Published digitally at earlymodernletters.org. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

div.p: I wrote by the post of Sandwich but what meanes yt made to get over I know not. I thancke you, but specially for the letter of the 25 of the last, for which your skipper brought me yours so full of perticulers which the winter weather affoordes. Here are no great novelties for Ireland, but the whole plot and the erle of Essex his jorny thether is neither fast nor loose but holdes still in suspence, by reason the proportions thought fit for such an enterprise are dayly clipt and deminished; for yt was first set downe that his number (with those that be there alredy) shold be 14000 and so to be continued from time to time with full allowaunce of vitayle, mony, and other charge aunswerable, but they thincke the matter may be compassed with lesse, and on these termes yt standes, wherewith he is nothing pleased. For eight or ten dayes the souldiers flockt about him and every man hoped to be a colonell at least, and to content theyre humors somwhat yt was said there shold be petty regiments raised of 500 and 300 men. The erle of Southampton was named to be generall of the horse, Sir Rob. Sidney to be Lord Marshall, and I know not how many more to other places; but now all is husht again and only Sir Arthur SavageSir Arthur Savage (d. 1631), soldier and MP; served in Ireland in various capacities through the Nine Years' War. He was among the few officers who actually proceeded to Ireland in late 1598, ahead of Essex's main expedition the following year. is going thether with a thowsand men, and the erle of TomondDonough O'Brien (c. 1560–1624), fourth Earl of Thomond; one of the few Gaelic-descended Irish magnates who remained consistently loyal to the English crown throughout the Nine Years' War. His appointment as governor of Limerick gave the English a secure foothold in Munster. with some other small forces to governor of Limricke. Here is order geven for 2000 men to be sent out of Kent, Surrey, and Sussex into the Lowe Countries, and to be drawne from thence out of the garrison for Ireland; and for horsemen to that service the justices of peace throughout England must be intreated to furnish them.

But the greatest difficultie is mony, which goes so lowe that for all the subsidies comming on, the privy seales that are past,Privy seals were letters under the Privy Seal directing wealthy individuals to lend specified sums to the crown — effectively a forced loan mechanism. Combined with parliamentary subsidies, they were the principal instruments of Elizabethan war finance. and the loane of 20000li the other day, the Quene is now in hand to borrow of the Citie 150000li, offering to lay 5000li of her land in morgage, and the customes of the citie of London for the repayment of yt, and the marchaunts and aldermen of London are sent for and dealt with severally and urged to lend some 3000li, some 2000li, some more, some lesse; but howsoever yt fall out, yea though they were willing, I hardly thincke yt is to be had, for the citie is much decayed by reason theyre trafike is greatly impaired, and the marchant adventurers were almost upon dissolving, being hindered in theyre trade by certain enterlopers against whom they could have no remedy. Many men muse how the Quene, being discharged of the burthen of the Lowe Countries, and having no other charge but Ireland, should be driven to these straights, and unles there shold be exceding urgent need indeed, yt is thought a straunge pollicie to discover our want so far to the world.Chamberlain's point is a political one: public knowledge of the crown's financial straits would encourage England's enemies abroad and embolden the Irish rebels. Revealing fiscal weakness was itself a strategic liability. The Lord Buckhurst is now in full cry to be Lord Treasurer, and yt is thought the wardesThe Mastership of the Court of Wards and Liveries, the most lucrative royal office available, still contested between Essex and others; see Letters 9–11. will fall to Master Secretary, yet some say the Quene will bestow yt upon some meane man who for a pension shall execute the place to her best advantage. Yt was said awhile that the erle of Essex should have 6000li yeerely out of yt, otherwhile that he shold have 20000li toward the paiment of his debts and so to leave his hold, but we see neither come on very fast. He kept a kinde of marshalls court of late where the title of Sir NevillEdward Neville (c. 1556–1618), who claimed the barony of Abergavenny through the female line. The hearing concerned whether he was entitled to be summoned to Parliament as Lord Abergavenny; he was eventually so summoned in 1604. that claimes to be Lord of Abergeny, and the title of Sir Henry LeonardSir Henry Leonard (d. 1626), son of Sampson Leonard and Margaret, suo jure Baroness Dacre. His claim to the barony of Dacre of the South was ultimately resolved in his favour; he succeeded as twelfth Lord Dacre in 1612. that wold be Lord Dacres of the South was argued, but after divers hearings he left the matter as he found yt, and so yt is referred to the Quene.

We heare that Sir Ant. SherleySir Anthony Sherley (1565–1636?), adventurer and self-styled diplomat. He had set out for Persia in 1598 on an unauthorised mission to the Shah, passing through the Ottoman Empire and the Levant. At Constantinople and Aleppo he extorted money from English merchants by issuing bills of exchange drawn on Essex — without any authorisation to do so. His subsequent wanderings made him notorious across Europe. hath ben at Constantinople and there wrounge out of our marchants 400li, and from thence he went to Aleppo and there scraped together 500li more, wherewith he hath charged billes by the Lord of Essex his name, and so is gon on God knowes whether to seeke his fortune. Sir Richard LewsonSir Richard Leveson (c. 1570–1605), naval commander; son of Sir Walter Leveson of Lilleshall, Shropshire. He distinguished himself in later actions against Spanish shipping and became Vice Admiral of England in 1604. hath lately staide 17 saile of Easterlings and Hollanders in the narrow seas that were going for Spaine; in the mean time his fatherSir Walter Leveson of Lilleshall, Shropshire; detained in the Fleet prison by creditors who had laid a trap for him and caught him at Lambeth. is staide in the Fleet, being faln into hucksters handes (I mean his old creditors) who laide a traine for him and caught him at Lambeth. The Lord MarquisWilliam Paulet (c. 1532–1598), third Marquis of Winchester. He died on 25 November 1598 and was succeeded by his son William, fourth Marquis. is lately dead having left a noble sonne to succeed him, who is like to prove as sufficient a man as none of them all. Master ParkerA brother of Edward Parker, tenth Lord Morley (1550–1618). The slain man's given name is not recorded. brother to the Lord Morley was slaine the last weeke by a man of Sir Tho. Gerrards about a brabble the way, wherof the fellow is acquitted, being found he did yt in his owne defence.

The French inventairieInventaire Général de l'Histoire de France (Paris, 1597) by Jean de Serres (c. 1540–1598), a comprehensive history of France; the author died before completing the work. is not come foorth, the author being saide to be dead, but there is hope yt wilbe found among his papers. Here is nothing come out this last martThe Frankfurt book fair (Buchmesse), held twice yearly and the main international book market. Chamberlain regularly reported on what was available at Frankfurt to Carleton, who collected books. worth the looking after; for that many thinges are currant there that be here forbidden, I send you likewise such pedlarie pamflets and threehalfpeny ware as we are served with, and I do not thincke but you may fit yourself better at Middleburg, but make the best use you can of them and use your owne censure; but yf I be not deceved some of the satires are passable. You shall receve by this bearer your Ortelius his Thesaurus Geographicus,Finally dispatched after being held up since Letter 10, when it was judged too large for ordinary post. It is sent by a special bearer, with Rowland Lytton insisting on paying for it. your cousen Lytton will needs bestow yt on you, wherin I made no longe strife. Our Mistris AnneAnne Lytton, probably a daughter or close relation of Rowland Lytton of Knebworth. An old doctor of law who had long kept her in expectation of marriage died without fulfilling his promise, leaving her a widow before she was a wife. is become a widow before she was a wife, an old Doctor of Law that hath held her long in hand to marry her being dead, whereupon she mournes in her chamber. And Master SherleyGeorge Shirley (c. 1559–1622), an acquaintance of Chamberlain's and Carleton's circles; previously mentioned in Letter 5. hath at last married the Lady UmptonDorothy Unton (née Wroughton; d. 1634), widow of Sir Henry Unton (1558–1596), the diplomat and subject of the celebrated memorial portrait now in the National Portrait Gallery. Chamberlain's sardonic remark — that "as poor a man as I am I would not buy such another of the price" — implies Lady Unton had a reputation for being difficult. and made her his deere wife; for mine owne part as poore a man as I am I wold not buy such another of the price. Doctor Parkins is come out of Denmarke, and sayes he hath brought a goode aunswer. And thus wishing you all health and welfare I end for this time.

postscript.p: To my very goode frend Master Dudley Carleton attending on the Lord Governor of Ostend geve these.
